title: Hailey Hutch for Twin and Full Beds
summary: An open-cubby hutch that mounts above a storage-bed headboard — one shallow box, sized to twin or full, no drawers to fuss with.
description:
This is a shallow, plywood-backed box that sits on top of a headboard and gives you open cubby storage right at pillow height — books, a lamp, a phone charger, whatever a nightstand usually holds but out of the way of a bed with no floor space beside it. It's designed as a companion to a storage-bed headboard, so the two size options (twin and full) match those bed widths exactly; measure your actual headboard before cutting; the brief itself calls this a tight fit.

Construction is dead simple: a plywood-backed box with seven evenly-spaced dividers that create the cubby openings, trimmed out front and sides with 1x2/1x3 so the plywood edges don't show. Build it as a nailed-and-glued box if you have a brad nailer, or use screws throughout if you don't — either way, check for square before the glue sets, because a shallow box like this has almost no depth to hide a racked frame.

tools.note: Fast way to tack the plywood back and trim while the glue sets. Screws work too, just slower.
Used when you mount the finished hutch above the headboard.

materials.name: 1x12 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Top, bottom, and the seven cubby dividers.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, hardboard, or beadboard, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Back panel — any of the three works; beadboard reads more finished.

materials.name: Wood screws, 2"
materials.note: Optional, for anyone building this without a nailer.

cutList.note: Use these lengths for a twin headboard; see full-size lengths below.

steps.title: Build the box
steps.body:
Cut the top, bottom, and dividers to length for whichever size (twin or full) matches your headboard, then assemble into a simple box: dividers sandwiched between the top and bottom boards, evenly spaced to create equal cubby openings.

Glue every joint and, if you have a nailer, tack it together with 1-1/4" finish nails while the glue sets — otherwise pilot-drill and drive 2" screws. Check the box for square with a diagonal measurement before you move on; there's no frame to hide a racked box behind here.

steps.title: Attach the plywood back
steps.body: Cut the 1/4" back panel to match the outside dimensions of the box and nail it on with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue, keeping edges flush. The back is what keeps the whole thing square long-term, so make sure it's actually cut square before you tack it down.

steps.title: Trim the front
steps.body: Attach the 1x2 top and bottom trim across the front face, then the 1x3 along the very bottom edge, all with 1-1/4" finish nails and glue. This covers the raw edges of the top/bottom boards and gives the front a finished face-frame look.

steps.title: Trim the sides
steps.body: Add the two side trim pieces the same way, flush to the top and bottom of the box.

steps.title: Sand and mount
steps.body:
Sand every edge, especially the cubby openings — hands and books go in and out of these constantly. Set the hutch on top of your headboard, check it's level side to side, and screw down through the bottom into the headboard framing.

Measure your actual headboard width before you cut anything: this is a tight, matched fit by design, not a generic shelf.

images.alt: Open cubby hutch mounted above a storage bed headboard
